common_name: Corno di Toro Pepper (Bull's Horn Italian Frying Pepper, Sweet and Thin-Walled for Roasting)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Corno di Toro (Italian for 'Bull's Horn') refers to the distinctive elongated, curved form of the ripe pepper, which resembles a bovine horn. Both red and yellow ripening strains are grown in Italy and available in North America. In zone 9, transplant March–April for summer production; the variety produces vigorously through the Gulf Coast summer and into fall. The thin walls and long curved form make Corno di Toro the ideal roasting pepper: the thin flesh chars and caramelizes quickly under high heat, and the long shape provides a good ratio of skin to flesh. Harvest green for mild flavor or allow to ripen to full red or yellow for maximum sweetness. In Italian cuisine, Corno di Toro is one of the most important culinary peppers — used grilled, fried in olive oil, stuffed, and in antipasto — distinct from bell peppers in texture, sweetness, and cooking properties.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 75–80°F; transplant after last frost. Harvest green or fully ripe. Corno di Toro (Bull's Horn Pepper) is one of Italy's most beloved culinary peppers — a sweet, thin-walled frying and roasting pepper grown throughout southern and central Italy and named for the long, tapering, curved shape of the ripe fruit that resembles a bull's horn. The typical fruit is 6–10 inches long, 1.5–2 inches wide at the shoulder, tapering to a point, with a slight irregular curve along the length — distinctly different in shape from both the blocky bell pepper and the narrow frying types like Jimmy Nardello. Red-ripening ('Corno di Toro Rosso') and yellow-ripening ('Corno di Toro Giallo') strains are both traditional in Italy and both produce sweet, mild fruit with no heat. The thin walls are the key culinary property: Corno di Toro lacks the thick, watery flesh of bell peppers and has instead a thinner, more concentrated flesh layer that caramelizes deeply and quickly under high cooking heat — roasted in a hot oven or over open flame, the peppers char and soften to a silky, intensely sweet texture that is quite different from the same cooking treatment applied to thick-walled bell peppers. In Italian antipasto tradition, roasted Corno di Toro peppers are dressed with olive oil, garlic, and capers, or sautéed in olive oil until very soft and served as a condiment with grilled meat. The variety has been grown by Italian-American gardeners in the United States for generations and has become more widely available through specialty seed companies.
sources: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Corno di Toro Italian frying pepper variety notes
Seed Savers Exchange — Corno di Toro red and yellow heirloom Italian pepper documentation
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Italian frying pepper variety comparison

common_name: Carmen Pepper (AAS-Winning Italian Frying Pepper, Early-Producing and Heat-Tolerant)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Carmen is an F1 hybrid Italian frying pepper developed for superior earliness and productivity compared to heirloom Italian frying pepper types like Corno di Toro. In zone 9, transplant March–April; Carmen performs well through the Gulf Coast summer and fall. As an Italian frying pepper type, Carmen produces elongated (4–6 inches), slightly blocky-to-tapered fruits that ripen from green to bright red. The fruits are meaty, sweet, and thin-walled — more uniform in shape and earlier to ripen than heirloom Italian types. Carmen received All-America Selections (AAS) recognition in 2006, confirmed as performing well across diverse North American growing conditions. The early maturity of Carmen compared to heirloom frying types makes it practical for zone 9's spring production window before summer heat limits continued fruit set.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 75–80°F; transplant after last frost. Harvest green or fully red for best flavor. Carmen pepper is an F1 hybrid Italian frying pepper that received the All-America Selections recognition in 2006 for its consistent performance across varied North American growing conditions — specifically for its combination of earliness (reaching harvest stage significantly sooner than traditional Corno di Toro types), productivity (high fruit set per plant), and fruit quality. The All-America Selections program evaluates new vegetable and flower varieties across multiple trial locations nationwide, and a 2006 AAS designation reflects verified performance advantages over competing varieties at the time of release. Carmen's fruits are medium-large (4–6 inches long, 2 inches wide), with a form intermediate between a large Cubanelle and a Corno di Toro: longer and more pointed than a Cubanelle, but somewhat less dramatically curved and elongated than a classic Corno di Toro. The flesh is thick enough to provide substance for roasting and stuffing but still thinner-walled and more flavorful at full ripeness than standard bell peppers. The ripening process in Carmen goes through green to light orange to fully red — a complete color change that signals maximum sweetness and vitamin C content. Carmen's Italian frying pepper lineage gives it the characteristic sweetness and frying quality that makes this pepper type so valued in Mediterranean cooking: roasted Carmen peppers develop a silky, caramelized richness that is genuinely different from roasted bell peppers. For zone 9 gardeners, the early-producing quality of Carmen means productive harvests before midsummer heat stress limits continued flower and fruit production.
sources: All-America Selections — Carmen pepper 2006 AAS winner recognition
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Carmen Italian frying pepper F1 hybrid notes
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Italian Frying Pepper Variety Comparison

common_name: Aconcagua Pepper (Argentine Giant Sweet Frying Pepper, 12-Inch Fruits with Mild Flavor)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Aconcagua is a large, late-season frying pepper requiring 75–90 days from transplant to reach full development — the largest fruits take the most time. In zone 9, transplant March–April for summer production; the variety's heat tolerance allows continued production through Gulf Coast summers, and the late season ripening means peak harvests often come in July–August. Provide large containers (5-gallon minimum) if growing in pots — the tall (3–4 foot) plants and large fruit weight require good stability. Stake or cage plants to support the fruit load. Aconcagua is named for the highest peak in the Andes Mountains in Argentina, referencing the variety's South American origin. Harvest green for mild use or fully ripened to salmon-red for sweetest flavor and full carotenoid development.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 75–80°F; transplant after last frost. Stake plants; harvest green or fully ripe to salmon-red. Aconcagua is an Argentine heirloom sweet frying pepper that produces some of the largest sweet pepper fruits of any variety in home garden cultivation — individual fruits typically measuring 8–12 inches in length and 2–3 inches wide, with a conical to slightly flattened form that tapers to a point. The variety takes its name from Aconcagua, Argentina's (and the Americas') highest mountain, a reference to the pepper's origin in the Andean region of South America where diverse pepper varieties have been cultivated for millennia. The fruit size is genuinely dramatic: Aconcagua peppers dwarf most other sweet peppers in the garden and attract immediate attention at farmers markets and in garden displays where their unusual scale distinguishes them from any standard frying or bell pepper. Despite the imposing size, the walls are moderately thin (thinner than bell peppers, in the frying pepper tradition) and the flavor is mild, sweet, and fruity — the flesh has the pleasant, slightly aromatic quality characteristic of good heirloom frying peppers. The ripening progression of Aconcagua goes from green through light yellow-green to a distinctive salmon-pink to pale red-orange, a color not typical of most peppers and part of the variety's visual distinction. The large individual fruit size makes Aconcagua exceptionally practical for stuffing (the hollow interior accommodates substantial filling) and for roasting whole, where the large peppers yield significant amounts of sweet roasted flesh per fruit. The variety is open-pollinated and seed-saving is straightforward from fully ripe fruits.
sources: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Aconcagua giant sweet pepper Argentine variety documentation
Seed Savers Exchange — Aconcagua heirloom pepper variety notes
Seeds of Change — Aconcagua sweet frying pepper variety description

common_name: White Velvet Okra (Smooth Round-Pod Heirloom Okra with Velvety Texture)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is thoroughly warm (70°F minimum); White Velvet okra produces round, pale green to cream-white pods rather than the elongated, ridged form of standard okra. In zone 9, plant April through July for summer production. Soak seeds overnight before planting to improve germination. The pods are smooth and rounded in cross-section (unlike the distinctly ribbed, angular pods of most standard okra) — the 'round' pod character reduces toughness at larger sizes, allowing harvest at 3–4 inches without the fibrous, over-mature texture that makes standard okra unpalatable when left too long. White Velvet has an old, well-documented history in American seed saving and is available through heirloom seed companies. The slightly different pod form and smooth surface give the cooked pods a different texture than standard okra — less 'slimy' than some types, which some cooks prefer.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after soil warms to 70°F; thin to 12–18 inches. Harvest pods at 2–3 inches for most tender pods. White Velvet Okra is an heirloom okra variety distinguished from the standard elongated ridged types by its round, smooth pods — the 'velvet' texture of the surface, which lacks the prominent longitudinal ridges of varieties like Clemson Spineless, and the pale green to cream-white color at harvest maturity (before fully ripening to cream-white and drying). The round-pod okra group, of which White Velvet is one of the best-known American heirloom examples, represents a different selection from the standard commercial okra breeding that prioritized uniformly ridged, elongated pods for mechanical harvesting and packing. Round-pod types have thinner, more tender pod walls and a slightly different texture and mucilage content than ridged types — attributes that some cooks prefer, particularly for preparations where the 'sliminess' of okra is considered a negative quality. White Velvet's pods harvested at 2–3 inches have a tender, delicate texture and mild flavor appropriate for sautéing, grilling, pickling, and use in gumbo where the mucilaginous properties of okra serve as a natural thickener. The plant is vigorous and heat-tolerant — essential qualities for zone 9 summer production — and produces prolifically throughout the summer if harvested regularly. The cream-white color at harvest maturity (before pods fully dry and tan) makes White Velvet visually distinctive from the dark green and burgundy okra varieties and attractive for fresh-market presentation.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — White Velvet round-pod heirloom okra documentation
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — White Velvet okra historical variety notes
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Heritage okra variety descriptions for the South

common_name: Green Star Okra (High-Yield Compact Hybrid Okra for Commercial and Home Production)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil has warmed to 70°F minimum; Green Star is a compact F1 hybrid okra variety with a shorter internode spacing (reduced distance between leaves and pod sets) than standard tall varieties, producing more pods per foot of plant height and remaining productive at 4–5 feet compared to standard varieties that can reach 8–10 feet. In zone 9, plant April through July for summer production. The compact plant habit of Green Star makes it easier to manage in home gardens and more efficient in raised-bed and small-garden situations where standard tall okra varieties would overgrow their space. Harvest pods at 3–4 inches — Green Star's pods stay tender at slightly larger sizes than some varieties due to its spineless, smooth pod texture. Regular harvesting (every 2–3 days at peak production) is essential to maintain pod quality and stimulate continued production.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after soil warms; thin to 12–18 inches. Harvest at 3–4 inches every 2–3 days. Green Star is an F1 hybrid okra variety selected for compact plant habit, high productivity per plant, and good pod quality — attributes that make it one of the more practical hybrid okra varieties for small-scale and home garden production in the Gulf South. The hybrid vigor of F1 varieties (first-generation crosses between distinct parental lines) typically produces improved uniformity, vigor, and productivity compared to open-pollinated varieties, and Green Star's compact growth habit — reaching 4–5 feet rather than the 6–10 feet of some tall heirloom types — makes it easier to harvest without a ladder and fits better in standard garden plots. The pods are dark green, slightly ridged in the standard okra style, spineless to low-spine (important for comfort in harvesting), and tender and good-flavored at the 3–4 inch harvest stage. Green Star's earliness — first harvest approximately 50–55 days from direct seeding — is competitive with the fastest-maturing okra varieties and allows harvest to begin before the peak of Gulf Coast summer heat. Okra is one of the most important and culturally significant vegetables in Gulf South cooking: a staple of gumbo (where it serves as a natural thickener via its mucilaginous properties), fried okra (a Southern culinary tradition), pickled okra, and fresh vegetable side dishes. Zone 9 is ideal for okra production — the combination of heat and humidity that limits most cool-season vegetables is precisely what okra requires to thrive.
sources: Harris Seeds — Green Star okra F1 hybrid commercial performance notes
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Compact okra variety comparison for home and market gardens
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Okra Variety Selection and Production for the Gulf South
